figures.caption: Figure 1: An example of CQR. Green and blue represent the key information for retrieval that was omitted in Q3.
Table 1: Evaluation results of various retrieval system types on the test sets of TopiOCQA and QReCC, where all reported values of the baselines originated from their papers.
Figure 6: The prompt used to construct the response preference data based on retrieval.
Figure 7: The trend of R@100 on TopiOCQA with the change in the number of sampled candidate rewritten queries.
Figure 2: An overview of MSPA-CQR, including the two stages of Multi-Faceted Preference Data Construction and Prefix-Guided Multi-Faceted Preference Optimization.
Table 15: A case study on QReCC.
Table 14: A case study on QReCC.
Table 6: Zero-shot retrieval performances under the dense retrieval (ANCE).

Figure 4: A comparison of the linguistic features of rewritten queries generated under three preference guides. “N-grams div.” refers to the diversity of n-grams (n = 2), and “Edit dist.” refers to the Levenshtein distance.
Table 13: A case study on TopiOCQA.
Figure 9: A case of merging multiple rewritten queries into a single query.
Table 4: A case study on TopiOCQA, where “Rank” refers to the ranking of the retrieved gold passage, and “Not Found” means that there is no gold passage in the top-100 retrieved passages.
Figure 8: The trend of MRR on TopiOCQA with the change in the number of sampled candidate rewritten queries.
